_purpose: Local economy timeline for the TN East Great Valley regional cluster (Washington, Sullivan, Greene, Carter, Hawkins, Cocke, Grainger, Jefferson counties). Covers 1770-1960 across six economic eras — from the Watauga frontier through the TVA era. East Tennessee's economy was defined by its position: fertile valley floor for agriculture, mountain barriers to commerce, fierce independence that resisted both plantation slavery and industrial domination. The region was yeoman country from settlement to industrialization — small farms, modest slaveholding, and a market economy connected to Virginia and the world beyond by the Great Valley corridor and, after 1858, by the railroad.
display_name: TN East Great Valley — Local Economy Timeline

economic_eras.era_name: Frontier Settlement and the Watauga Economy
economic_eras.dominant_economy: Subsistence farming on Cherokee cession lands; livestock herding on open range; salt lick exploitation; fur and deerskin trade declining; small-scale milling at waterpower sites
economic_eras.description: The East Tennessee economy began at the Watauga Settlement (1772), where Scots-Irish families from Virginia and the Carolina Piedmont established farms on Cherokee lands acquired by treaty and force. The economy was subsistence-oriented: corn, wheat, hogs, and cattle, with surplus traded at frontier stores for salt, iron, and gunpowder. The fur trade had declined by the 1770s but still provided cash income. Salt licks — natural brine springs — were critical resources; the one at present-day Saltville, Virginia, connected the region to a vital supply chain. The Revolutionary War disrupted settlement but the frontier economy recovered quickly as land grants for military service brought more settlers. The State of Franklin (1784-1788) was partly an economic venture — an attempt by settlers to control their own land distribution and tax revenue.

county_specifics.note: The Watauga Settlement was the economic origin point. Jonesborough (est. 1779) was the first town — courthouse, general store, blacksmith. Subsistence farming on limestone valley soils.
Long Island of the Holston was the staging point for westward trade and migration. The Great Valley corridor connected Sullivan to Virginia markets.
Settled in the 1780s. Andrew Johnson's later home. Farming economy on the Nolichucky valley floor.
Mountain frontier — the most rugged terrain in the cluster. Subsistence economy on narrow valley floors.
Gateway to Cumberland Gap. Dual economy: settled farming in the valley, transit trade from westward migrants.
French Broad valley settlement. Most isolated county in the cluster. Subsistence economy from the start.
Formed 1796. Productive valley farmland between Clinch Mountain and the Holston.
Formed 1792. Convergence of French Broad and Holston created a natural crossroads. Early market town at Dandridge.

key_infrastructure.name: Watauga Settlement trading post
key_infrastructure.impact: First permanent European commercial activity in what became Tennessee. Established the economic geography that persists.

key_infrastructure.name: Great Valley Road (Virginia extension into TN)
key_infrastructure.impact: The migration and trade corridor that connected East Tennessee to Virginia markets. All commerce flowed northeast through this corridor.

key_infrastructure.name: Frontier grist and saw mills
key_infrastructure.impact: Waterpower mills at river falls and creek junctions provided the first processing infrastructure. Mill sites became town sites.

wage_benchmarks.source: Wright, Gavin. Old South, New South, ch. 1

economic_eras.narrative_hook: In the {decade}s, {ancestor_name} worked land in {county} County that had been Cherokee territory within a generation — cleared by hand, planted between stumps, and defended with a rifle that was as much a farming tool as the plow.
economic_eras.source: Finger, John R. Tennessee Frontiers: Three Regions in Transition. Bloomington: Indiana University Press, 2001. Williams, Samuel Cole. History of the Lost State of Franklin. Johnson City: Watauga Press, 1924.

economic_eras.era_name: Yeoman Valley Economy and the State of Franklin Legacy
economic_eras.dominant_economy: Diversified yeoman agriculture — corn, wheat, livestock, orchards; small-scale slaveholding; iron ore in Carter County area; droving livestock to Virginia and Lowcountry markets; salt trade; early zinc mining in Jefferson County
economic_eras.description: The antebellum East Tennessee economy was fundamentally yeoman — small to medium farms (50-200 acres), diversified crops, modest slaveholding (most slaveholders held fewer than five enslaved people), and deep integration with Virginia markets through the Great Valley corridor. This was NOT plantation country. The ratio of enslaved to free population in these eight counties was far below the Middle and West Tennessee average. The economy was commercial but small-scale: farmers sold surplus grain, livestock, and orchard products to local merchants who shipped through the valley to Virginia or south to Knoxville. The livestock drove — herding hogs and cattle to Virginia markets — was a significant cash activity. Iron ore deposits in the Carter County/Cranberry area were beginning to be exploited. Zinc deposits in Jefferson County were identified but not yet commercially mined at scale.

county_specifics.note: Established agricultural county. Jonesborough a prosperous market town. Slaveholding modest — approximately 15% of families.
Valley farming and transit trade to Virginia. Blountville and the Bristol area growing as commercial centers.
Prosperous farming county. Andrew Johnson's political base. Slaveholding below the state average.
Mountain subsistence economy supplemented by iron ore deposits at Cranberry (across the NC line). Poorest county in the cluster.
Broad valley farming. Gateway to Cumberland Gap migration corridor. Rogersville developing as a market town.
Isolated mountain economy. French Broad valley farming on narrow bottomland. Whiskey production already established.
Quiet agricultural prosperity. Clinch Mountain valley farming.
Growing commercial center at Dandridge. Zinc ore deposits identified. Carson-Newman founded 1851.

key_infrastructure.name: Livestock drove roads to Virginia
key_infrastructure.impact: Annual fall drives moved thousands of hogs and cattle northeast through the Great Valley to Virginia and Carolina markets. The drove was the primary cash-generating activity for valley farmers.

key_infrastructure.name: Cranberry iron mines (Carter County border)
key_infrastructure.impact: Iron ore deposits at Cranberry (now Avery County, NC) were accessed through Carter County. Early mining operations employed local labor.

key_infrastructure.name: Salt works connections to Saltville, VA
key_infrastructure.impact: Salt was essential for meat preservation. The Saltville, VA works supplied East Tennessee through the Great Valley corridor.

major_employers.name: Yeoman farms (50-200 acres)
major_employers.employment_scale: Dominant employment — nearly every family farmed

major_employers.name: Cranberry iron mines and related operations
major_employers.employment_scale: Scores of workers — small but significant for the local economy

wage_benchmarks.source: Wright, Old South, New South, ch. 1
Eller, Miners, Millhands, and Mountaineers, ch. 2
Wright, Old South, New South, ch. 1

economic_eras.narrative_hook: In {county} County in the {decade}s, {ancestor_name} farmed valley land that grew corn, wheat, and apples — not plantation country, not poverty country, but the yeoman middle ground where a family with fifty acres and a good spring could live independent and vote free.
economic_eras.source: Inscoe, John C. Mountain Masters, Slavery, and the Sectional Crisis in Western North Carolina. Knoxville: University of Tennessee Press, 1989. Finger, Tennessee Frontiers, chs. 5-6.

economic_eras.dominant_economy: Agricultural disruption; guerrilla war destroying infrastructure; salt shortage; Confederate conscription draining labor; Union enlistments from occupied territory; bridge burning and reprisals; barter economy replacing cash
economic_eras.description: East Tennessee's Civil War experience was unique in the Confederacy. The region voted overwhelmingly against secession (the counties in this cluster voted 70-85% Unionist in the February 1861 referendum), was occupied by Confederate forces against its will, and became a guerrilla war zone for four years. The economy collapsed. Confederate conscription removed men from farms. Union sympathizers were arrested, hanged, or fled to Kentucky to join Union regiments. The bridge-burning conspiracy of November 1861 — coordinated destruction of East Tennessee & Virginia Railroad bridges — brought severe Confederate reprisals, including executions. Salt became unobtainable (the Saltville works were in Confederate Virginia). The barter economy replaced cash. Farms were stripped by foraging armies on both sides. The most productive valley farmland was trampled by military movements between Knoxville and Virginia.

county_specifics.note: Strongly Unionist. Bridge burning conspirators from this area. Confederate reprisals included hangings.
Battle of Blountville (1863). Divided loyalties — some Confederate support along the Virginia border.
Strongly Unionist. Andrew Johnson's home county — his family suffered Confederate harassment. General John Hunt Morgan killed in Greeneville (1864).
Unionist stronghold. Mountain terrain provided refuge for men evading Confederate conscription.
Divided — valley families more Confederate-leaning, mountain families more Unionist. Military movements through the county.
Unionist mountain county. Guerrilla activity throughout the war. Confederate and Union bushwhackers both operated.
Unionist. Farming disrupted by military foraging and conscription.
Divided loyalties. Strategic location between Knoxville and the upper valley made it a transit corridor for both armies.

key_infrastructure.name: East Tennessee & Virginia Railroad (military target)
key_infrastructure.impact: The ET&V Railroad was the critical supply line connecting Virginia to Knoxville. Bridge burning (November 1861) and military damage disrupted service repeatedly. The railroad was the reason the Confederacy could not afford to let East Tennessee secede from the Confederacy.

key_infrastructure.name: Salt supply cutoff
key_infrastructure.impact: Loss of access to Virginia salt works created a preservation crisis. Families could not cure meat for winter. Substitute salt sources (boiling brine from salt licks) were inadequate.

major_employers.name: Confederate military service (forced)
major_employers.employment_scale: Confederate conscription swept up men regardless of loyalty

major_employers.name: Union military service (voluntary)
major_employers.employment_scale: Thousands of East TN men fled to Kentucky to enlist in Union regiments — particularly after 1863 when Union forces controlled parts of the region

economic_eras.narrative_hook: East Tennessee voted against secession and fought it — and {ancestor_name} lived through the consequences: conscription into an army {pronoun_subject} did not believe in, or flight to Kentucky to join the Union, or hiding in the mountains while both sides burned farms and hanged neighbors.
economic_eras.source: Fisher, Noel C. War at Every Door: Partisan Politics and Guerrilla Violence in East Tennessee, 1860-1869. Chapel Hill: University of North Carolina Press, 1997. Groce, W. Todd. Mountain Rebels: East Tennessee Confederates and the Civil War, 1860-1870. Knoxville: University of Tennessee Press, 1999.

economic_eras.era_name: Reconstruction, Railroad Recovery, and the Tobacco Transition
economic_eras.dominant_economy: Agricultural recovery; transition from grain to tobacco as cash crop; railroad expansion connecting the valley to national markets; Cranberry iron mines expanding via narrow-gauge railroad; zinc mining developing in Jefferson County; timber extraction beginning in mountain counties; emergence of Johnson City as railroad junction town
economic_eras.description: East Tennessee's Reconstruction was less violent than the Deep South because Unionists controlled local government from the start — there was no Confederate restoration to overthrow. But the economic recovery was slow. Farms had been stripped, fences destroyed, livestock driven off, and labor disrupted. The transition to tobacco as the primary cash crop (replacing grain for Virginia and Lowcountry markets) began in the 1870s and accelerated through the 1890s. Burley tobacco suited the climate and soils of the valley floor, and the railroad provided access to national tobacco markets. Johnson City was founded in 1869 at the junction of the ET&V and the ET&NC railroads, becoming the commercial center of the upper Great Valley. The Cranberry iron mines expanded with the construction of the ET&WNC narrow-gauge railroad through Carter County's Doe River gorge. Zinc mining in Jefferson County began producing at commercial scale.

county_specifics.note: Jonesborough eclipsed by Johnson City (founded 1869). Railroad junction economy developing.
Bristol growing as border town and railroad junction with Virginia.
Tobacco becoming the primary cash crop. Andrew Johnson returned from the presidency and died in 1875.
ET&WNC Railroad through the Doe River gorge (1882) connecting to Cranberry iron mines. Industrial development in mountain terrain.
Agricultural recovery. Tobacco cultivation expanding. Rogersville remaining a market town.
Most isolated county. Timber operations beginning. Moonshine production increasing as a cash activity.
Agricultural recovery. Quiet farming county.
Zinc mining expanding. Dandridge remaining county seat while Jefferson City (Carson-Newman) growing.

key_infrastructure.name: East Tennessee & Virginia Railroad (rebuilt and expanded)
key_infrastructure.impact: Rebuilt after war damage. Consolidated into the Southern Railway system by 1894. Connected East Tennessee to national markets via the Great Valley corridor.

key_infrastructure.name: ET&WNC Railroad ('Tweetsie') through Doe River gorge
key_infrastructure.impact: Narrow-gauge railroad connecting Elizabethton (Carter County) to Cranberry iron mines (NC). Created an industrial corridor in mountain terrain.

key_infrastructure.name: Johnson City railroad junction
key_infrastructure.impact: Founded at the junction of the ET&V and ET&NC railroads. Became the commercial center of the upper Great Valley, eclipsing Jonesborough.

major_employers.name: Cranberry iron mines and ET&WNC Railroad

wage_benchmarks.source: Wright, Old South, New South, ch. 3
Eller, Miners, Millhands, and Mountaineers, ch. 3
Eller, Miners, Millhands, and Mountaineers, ch. 5

economic_eras.narrative_hook: After the war, {ancestor_name} came home to a farm stripped bare and rebuilt it — this time planting tobacco where the corn had been, because the railroad could carry leaf to market and the leaf paid better than grain ever had.
economic_eras.source: Fisher, War at Every Door. Eller, Miners, Millhands, and Mountaineers, chs. 3, 5.

economic_eras.era_name: Industrial Transformation: Rayon, Chemicals, and Planned Cities
economic_eras.dominant_economy: Tobacco farming in the valleys; industrial development at Bristol, Johnson City, Kingsport, and Elizabethton; timber extraction in mountain counties; rayon manufacturing at Elizabethton; Tennessee Eastman and Kingsport Press in Kingsport; zinc mining in Jefferson County; moonshine economy in Cocke County
economic_eras.description: The early twentieth century brought industrial transformation to parts of the cluster while leaving others in the agricultural or subsistence economy. The most dramatic change was Kingsport — a planned industrial city built from scratch starting in 1917, bringing Tennessee Eastman Company (chemicals, 1920), Kingsport Press (1922), and other industries to the Holston valley. Elizabethton gained Bemberg and Glanzstoff rayon plants (1926-1928), employing thousands of young women from surrounding mountain farms and provoking the Elizabethton Rayon Strike of 1929 — one of the earliest industrial strikes in the New South. Bristol developed as a commercial center and railroad hub straddling the Virginia line. Meanwhile, the agricultural counties (Greene, Hawkins, Grainger) continued their tobacco and farming economy, and the mountain counties (Cocke, Carter) experienced timber extraction and continued isolation.

county_specifics.note: Johnson City growing as a regional center. Veterans Administration hospital (Mountain Home) established 1903.
Kingsport planned industrial city (1917+). Tennessee Eastman, Kingsport Press. Bristol as commercial center. The most industrialized county in the cluster.
Agricultural county. Tobacco farming. Greeneville slow-growing compared to the industrial tri-cities.
Bemberg and Glanzstoff rayon plants at Elizabethton (1926). Elizabethton Rayon Strike (1929). ET&WNC Railroad still running to Cranberry.
Agricultural. Rogersville market town. Some industrial development along railroad.
Timber extraction. Moonshine economy. Most isolated county. Champion Paper mill pollution on Pigeon River from NC plant.
Agricultural. Beginning of tomato specialty farming.
Zinc mining continuing. Dandridge and Jefferson City growing modestly.

key_infrastructure.name: Kingsport planned industrial city
key_infrastructure.impact: Complete industrial city built from scratch — factories, worker housing, schools, churches. Drew workers from across the valley and mountains.

key_infrastructure.name: Tennessee Eastman Company (Kingsport)
key_infrastructure.impact: Chemical manufacturing — cellulose acetate, plastics, fibers. Became the region's largest employer. Later part of Eastman Kodak.

key_infrastructure.name: Bemberg and Glanzstoff rayon plants (Elizabethton)
key_infrastructure.impact: German-owned rayon manufacturing employing 3,000+ workers, mostly young women from mountain farms. Site of the 1929 strike.

wage_benchmarks.source: Tennessee Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Statistics

economic_eras.narrative_hook: By {year}, the Great Valley had two economies — the old tobacco and farming world where {ancestor_name}'s people had always worked, and the new factory world at Kingsport or Elizabethton where the wages were cash and the whistle blew at a time that had nothing to do with the sunrise.
economic_eras.source: Wolfe, Margaret Ripley. Kingsport, Tennessee: A Planned American City. Lexington: University Press of Kentucky, 1987. Hall, Jacquelyn Dowd. 'Disorderly Women.' JAH 73 (1986): 354-382.

economic_eras.era_name: TVA Era and the World War II Economy
economic_eras.dominant_economy: TVA dam construction and reservoir creation; wartime industrial expansion; Douglas Dam emergency construction; continued tobacco farming; out-migration from mountain counties; suburban growth around Johnson City, Kingsport, Bristol (Tri-Cities)
economic_eras.description: The Tennessee Valley Authority transformed the physical and economic landscape of the cluster. Cherokee Dam (1941) on the Holston flooded bottomland in Hawkins and Grainger counties. Douglas Dam (1943) on the French Broad — built in twelve months as a wartime emergency — flooded bottomland in Jefferson County and nearly drowned Dandridge. Watauga Dam (1949) flooded the upper Watauga valley in Carter County. The dams provided electric power and flood control but displaced farming families and submerged the best agricultural land in each county. The wartime industrial expansion at Kingsport (Tennessee Eastman produced explosives components) drew thousands of workers from the farming and mountain counties. The Tri-Cities (Johnson City, Kingsport, Bristol) emerged as a single metropolitan area by 1950. Meanwhile, mountain counties like Cocke continued to lose population as young people left for factory jobs.

county_specifics.note: Johnson City growing. VA hospital expanding. Suburban development.
Kingsport wartime boom — Tennessee Eastman producing for defense. Bristol stable. Tri-Cities metropolitan identity forming.
Agricultural continuity. Tobacco still the cash crop. Some out-migration to Tri-Cities factories.
Watauga Dam (1949) flooding upper valley. Rayon plants continuing. ET&WNC Railroad declining.
Cherokee Lake (1941) flooding Holston bottomland. Agricultural economy disrupted.
Out-migration accelerating. Timber exhausted. Mountain farms declining. Most economically depressed county in the cluster.
Cherokee Lake flooding bottomland. Tomato farming on higher terraces continuing.
Douglas Dam (1943) flooding French Broad bottomland. Dandridge saved by congressional intervention. Zinc mining declining.

key_infrastructure.name: Cherokee Dam (TVA)
key_infrastructure.impact: Holston River dam flooding bottomland in Hawkins and Grainger counties. Electricity and flood control at the cost of the best farmland.

key_infrastructure.name: Douglas Dam (TVA, wartime emergency)
key_infrastructure.impact: French Broad River dam built in twelve months. Flooded Jefferson County bottomland. Dandridge saved by congressional action.

key_infrastructure.name: Watauga Dam (TVA)
key_infrastructure.impact: Flooded upper Watauga valley in Carter County. Communities displaced, cemeteries relocated.

key_infrastructure.name: Tennessee Eastman wartime expansion
key_infrastructure.impact: Explosives component production drew thousands of workers to Kingsport. The largest single employer in the cluster.

major_employers.name: TVA dam construction projects
major_employers.employment_scale: Thousands of construction workers on each project

major_employers.name: Tobacco farming
major_employers.employment_scale: Declining but still significant in valley counties

economic_eras.narrative_hook: TVA gave the Great Valley electricity and took the bottomland in return — {ancestor_name} watched the water rise over the farm, the fence lines, and the family cemetery, and drove away with a government check that did not cover what generations had built.
economic_eras.source: Tennessee Valley Authority. Annual Reports, 1933-1960. Wolfe, Kingsport, Tennessee.

economic_turning_points.impact: First permanent European economic activity in what became Tennessee. Established the valley-floor farming economy.
Failed attempt at independent statehood — partly economic (control of land grants and taxation).

economic_turning_points.event: East Tennessee & Virginia Railroad reaches the valley
economic_turning_points.impact: Connected East Tennessee to Virginia and national markets by rail. Ended the region's dependence on drove roads and wagon trade.

economic_turning_points.event: East Tennessee votes against secession; occupied by Confederacy
economic_turning_points.impact: Four years of economic devastation — farms stripped, infrastructure destroyed, labor drained by conscription and flight.

economic_turning_points.event: ET&WNC Railroad through Doe River gorge
economic_turning_points.impact: Opened the Cranberry iron mines to commercial-scale extraction via Carter County.
Transformed the upper Great Valley from agricultural to industrial. Drew workers from across the cluster.
Mountain women's first major industrial labor action. Demonstrated the collision between traditional mountain independence and factory discipline.
First major TVA dam in the cluster. Flooded Holston bottomland, displaced farming families, provided electricity.

economic_turning_points.event: Douglas Dam completed in twelve months (wartime)
economic_turning_points.impact: The fastest TVA dam construction. Flooded French Broad bottomland in Jefferson County.

narrative_hooks: The Great Valley's economy followed the rivers — {ancestor_name}'s farm sat on bottomland that the Nolichucky or the Holston or the French Broad had built over centuries, and the crops that grew on it connected the family to markets in Virginia, Knoxville, and eventually the world.

East Tennessee was yeoman country — not plantation, not poverty, but the middle ground where {ancestor_name}'s fifty acres and a good spring meant independence, a vote, and enough corn to feed the family and the hogs.

The railroad changed everything it touched — when the ET&V reached {ancestor_name}'s valley, the two-week drove to Virginia became a two-day train ride, and the tobacco that had been worth growing became worth more.
TVA electricity was the future, and {ancestor_name}'s bottomland was the price — the dam gave the valley light and took the farms that had fed it.

Kingsport's smokestacks drew {ancestor_name} out of the farming life and into the factory — wages that came every week instead of once a year after the tobacco sold, in exchange for the whistle and the time clock.

The 1929 rayon strike in Elizabethton was led by mountain women who discovered that the same stubbornness that had held their grandmothers' farms together could shut down a factory — and {ancestor_name} was part of that discovery.
source_references: Finger, John R. Tennessee Frontiers: Three Regions in Transition. Bloomington: Indiana University Press, 2001.
Fisher, Noel C. War at Every Door: Partisan Politics and Guerrilla Violence in East Tennessee, 1860-1869. Chapel Hill: University of North Carolina Press, 1997.
Eller, Ronald D. Miners, Millhands, and Mountaineers: Industrialization of the Appalachian South, 1880-1930. Knoxville: University of Tennessee Press, 1982.
Wolfe, Margaret Ripley. Kingsport, Tennessee: A Planned American City. Lexington: University Press of Kentucky, 1987.
Hall, Jacquelyn Dowd. 'Disorderly Women: Gender and Labor Militancy in the Appalachian South.' Journal of American History 73 (1986): 354-382.
Wright, Gavin. Old South, New South: Revolutions in the Southern Economy since the Civil War. New York: Basic Books, 1986.
Inscoe, John C. Mountain Masters, Slavery, and the Sectional Crisis in Western North Carolina. Knoxville: University of Tennessee Press, 1989.
